vocabulary.en: a bomb made from a metal pipe filled with explosive material
vocabulary.excerpt: The man accused of placing pipe bombs in Washington, D.C. five years ago will not have his charges dropped.
vocabulary.example_cn: 五年前在华盛顿特区放置管状炸弹的男子不会被撤销指控。

vocabulary.en: having an important effect on a large part of something
vocabulary.excerpt: Trump's sweeping pardon for January 6 rioters also applied to him.
vocabulary.example_cn: 特朗普对1月6日暴乱者的全面赦免也适用于他。

vocabulary.en: a formal proposal made in a meeting or court
vocabulary.excerpt: A judge on Monday rejected a motion to dismiss the case.
vocabulary.example_cn: 周一，一名法官驳回了撤销此案的动议。

vocabulary.en: the pieces that are left after something has been destroyed
vocabulary.excerpt: A new video out of New Jersey showing the moment a roof of a supermarket collapsed two people were trapped under the debris.
vocabulary.example_cn: 新泽西州的一段新视频显示，一家超市屋顶坍塌，两人被困在废墟下。

vocabulary.en: falling in large amounts
vocabulary.excerpt: The roof collapsed after torrential rain in the area.
vocabulary.example_cn: 该地区暴雨过后屋顶坍塌。

vocabulary.en: a period when there is no light because the electricity supply has failed
vocabulary.excerpt: Cuba working to restore power after suffering a nationwide blackout on Monday.
vocabulary.example_cn: 古巴在周一遭遇全国性停电后正在努力恢复供电。

vocabulary.en: the basic systems and services that a country or organization uses in order to work effectively
vocabulary.excerpt: The country's aging electric infrastructure struggles to meet demand.
vocabulary.example_cn: 该国老化的电力基础设施难以满足需求。

vocabulary.en: the action of surrounding a place with soldiers or ships to stop people or goods from entering or leaving
vocabulary.excerpt: Cuba's energy crisis became worse after the U.S. issued a blockade on fuel shipments.
vocabulary.example_cn: 在美国对燃料运输实施封锁后，古巴的能源危机变得更加严重。

vocabulary.en: to defeat a team or person in a competition so that they no longer take part
vocabulary.excerpt: They lost to Belgium by a final score of four to one, eliminating them from the tournament in the Round of 16.
vocabulary.example_cn: 他们以4比1的最终比分输给了比利时，在16强赛中被淘汰出局。

vocabulary.en: to give official permission for someone to do something
vocabulary.excerpt: U.S. player, Folarin Balogun, was cleared to play.
vocabulary.example_cn: 美国球员弗拉林·巴洛贡被批准上场。

vocabulary.en: a card shown by the referee in football to indicate that a player is being sent off the field
vocabulary.excerpt: He was supposed to be suspended due to a red card.
vocabulary.example_cn: 他本应因红牌被停赛。

vocabulary.en: a fictional villain with extraordinary powers or influence
vocabulary.excerpt: Is the U.S. going to become the super villain, the sports villain of the World Cup?
vocabulary.example_cn: 美国会成为世界杯的超级反派、体育反派吗？

vocabulary.en: causing a lot of disagreement or argument
vocabulary.excerpt: It was a controversial call to start.
vocabulary.example_cn: 一开始这就是一个有争议的判罚。

vocabulary.en: a formal request to a higher authority to change a decision
vocabulary.excerpt: FIFA had no sense of appeal.
vocabulary.example_cn: 国际足联没有上诉机制。

vocabulary.en: the stage of a competition where the loser is eliminated
vocabulary.excerpt: It's the World Cup qualifier. It's the World Cup game. Of course, it's a knockout phase.
vocabulary.example_cn: 这是世界杯预选赛。这是世界杯比赛。当然是淘汰赛阶段。

vocabulary.en: feeling that you have too much to deal with
vocabulary.excerpt: They looked overwhelmed, kind of spooked from the beginning.
vocabulary.example_cn: 他们看起来不知所措，从一开始就有点害怕。

vocabulary.en: worried or nervous
vocabulary.excerpt: They were very rattled from the start of this game.
vocabulary.example_cn: 他们从比赛一开始就非常紧张。

vocabulary.en: the ability to keep increasing or developing
vocabulary.excerpt: Was this a momentum killer?
vocabulary.example_cn: 这是扼杀势头吗？

vocabulary.en: newly successful and behaving arrogantly
vocabulary.excerpt: This was an upstart team.
vocabulary.example_cn: 这是一支新崛起的队伍。

vocabulary.en: brave and determined
vocabulary.excerpt: They're the plucky underdog.
vocabulary.example_cn: 他们是勇敢的弱者。

vocabulary.en: a person or team that is expected to lose a competition
vocabulary.excerpt: They're the plucky underdog.
vocabulary.example_cn: 他们是勇敢的弱者。

vocabulary.en: very dangerous
vocabulary.excerpt: This is the most perilous situation for him.
vocabulary.example_cn: 这是他最危险的处境。

vocabulary.en: the quality of being honest and having strong moral principles
vocabulary.excerpt: The integrity of the tournament itself has never been questioned until now.
vocabulary.example_cn: 比赛本身的完整性直到现在才受到质疑。

vocabulary.en: dishonestly arranged to get a particular result
vocabulary.excerpt: Just like the election was rigged in 2020.
vocabulary.example_cn: 就像2020年的选举被操纵一样。

vocabulary.en: a benefit that is not deserved or is obtained dishonestly
vocabulary.excerpt: This guy is kind of throwing his weight around and creating unfair advantages.
vocabulary.example_cn: 这家伙有点滥用职权，制造不公平的优势。

vocabulary.en: the quality of being very important and deserving respect
vocabulary.excerpt: There's something about the sanctity of sport.
vocabulary.example_cn: 体育的神圣性有其道理。

vocabulary.en: the act of giving or receiving something valuable in exchange for influence or action
vocabulary.excerpt: FIFA 2022 bribery scandal.
vocabulary.example_cn: 国际足联2022年贿赂丑闻。

vocabulary.en: to try to make someone like you by praising them or doing things to please them
vocabulary.excerpt: This is a FIFA president Gianni Infantino who has gone out of his way to curry favor with President Trump.
vocabulary.example_cn: 这位国际足联主席詹尼·因凡蒂诺不遗余力地讨好特朗普总统。

vocabulary.en: extremely angry
vocabulary.excerpt: The Israelis are apoplectic.
vocabulary.example_cn: 以色列人暴跳如雷。

vocabulary.cn: 打破平衡，起决定性作用
vocabulary.en: to be the deciding factor in a situation
vocabulary.excerpt: The Israelis are worried will tip the balance of power in the region.
vocabulary.example_cn: 以色列人担心这会打破该地区的权力平衡。

sentences.en: The man accused of placing pipe bombs in Washington, D.C. five years ago will not have his charges dropped.
sentences.cn: 五年前被指控在华盛顿特区放置管状炸弹的男子不会被撤销指控。
sentences.structure: 主语（The man）+ 后置定语（accused of placing pipe bombs in Washington, D.C. five years ago）+ 谓语（will not have）+ 宾语（his charges dropped）
sentences.analysis: 过去分词短语作后置定语修饰主语，'have his charges dropped' 是 have + 宾语 + 过去分词结构，表示'使指控被撤销'。

sentences.en: A judge on Monday rejected a motion to dismiss the case after the defendant tried to argue that Trump's sweeping pardon for January 6 rioters also applied to him.
sentences.cn: 周一，一名法官驳回了撤销此案的动议，此前被告试图辩称特朗普对1月6日暴乱者的全面赦免也适用于他。
sentences.structure: 主句（A judge rejected a motion）+ 不定式短语（to dismiss the case）+ 时间状语从句（after the defendant tried to argue that...）
sentences.analysis: 不定式短语作定语修饰 motion；after 引导时间状语从句，其中 that 引导宾语从句作 argue 的宾语。

sentences.en: The judge says, the pardon does not apply because he had not yet been convicted of any crime when the pardon was issued.
sentences.cn: 法官说，赦免不适用，因为赦免发布时他尚未被判定犯有任何罪行。
sentences.structure: 主句（The judge says）+ 宾语从句（the pardon does not apply）+ 原因状语从句（because he had not yet been convicted of any crime）+ 时间状语从句（when the pardon was issued）
sentences.analysis: 宾语从句省略了 that；原因状语从句中包含时间状语从句，过去完成时表示'在赦免发布之前'。

sentences.en: A new video out of New Jersey showing the moment a roof of a supermarket collapsed two people were trapped under the debris.
sentences.cn: 新泽西州的一段新视频显示，一家超市屋顶坍塌，两人被困在废墟下。
sentences.structure: 主语（A new video）+ 后置定语（out of New Jersey）+ 现在分词短语（showing the moment）+ 同位语从句（a roof of a supermarket collapsed two people were trapped under the debris）
sentences.analysis: 现在分词短语作定语修饰 video；the moment 后接同位语从句，说明具体时刻。

sentences.en: Cuba working to restore power after suffering a nationwide blackout on Monday.
sentences.cn: 古巴在周一遭遇全国性停电后正在努力恢复供电。
sentences.structure: 主语（Cuba）+ 谓语（working to restore power）+ 时间状语（after suffering a nationwide blackout on Monday）
sentences.analysis: 现在分词短语作谓语，表示正在进行的动作；after 引导时间状语，动名词短语作宾语。

sentences.en: The island has experienced several blackouts as the country's aging electric infrastructure struggles to meet demand.
sentences.cn: 该岛经历了多次停电，因为该国老化的电力基础设施难以满足需求。
sentences.structure: 主句（The island has experienced several blackouts）+ 原因状语从句（as the country's aging electric infrastructure struggles to meet demand）
sentences.analysis: as 引导原因状语从句，struggles to meet demand 表示'难以满足需求'。

sentences.en: Cuba's energy crisis became worse after the U.S. issued a blockade on fuel shipments.
sentences.cn: 在美国对燃料运输实施封锁后，古巴的能源危机变得更加严重。
sentences.structure: 主句（Cuba's energy crisis became worse）+ 时间状语从句（after the U.S. issued a blockade on fuel shipments）
sentences.analysis: after 引导时间状语从句，表示主句动作发生在从句之后。

sentences.en: They lost to Belgium by a final score of four to one, eliminating them from the tournament in the Round of 16.
sentences.cn: 他们以4比1的最终比分输给了比利时，在16强赛中被淘汰出局。
sentences.structure: 主句（They lost to Belgium by a final score of four to one）+ 结果状语（eliminating them from the tournament in the Round of 16）
sentences.analysis: 现在分词短语作结果状语，表示输球的结果是淘汰。

sentences.en: The loss comes despite the fact that U.S. player, Folarin Balogun, was cleared to play, even though originally he was supposed to be suspended due to a red card.
sentences.cn: 尽管美国球员弗拉林·巴洛贡被批准上场，尽管他原本因红牌应被停赛，但球队还是输了。
sentences.structure: 主句（The loss comes）+ 让步状语（despite the fact that...）+ 让步状语从句（even though originally he was supposed to be suspended due to a red card）
sentences.analysis: despite 后接同位语从句；even though 引导让步状语从句，表示'尽管'。

sentences.en: And that suspension, of course, was lifted after a phone call from President Trump to the head of FIFA.
sentences.cn: 当然，在特朗普总统给国际足联主席打电话后，停赛被取消了。
sentences.structure: 主语（that suspension）+ 谓语（was lifted）+ 时间状语（after a phone call from President Trump to the head of FIFA）
sentences.analysis: 被动语态；after 引导时间状语，表示'在……之后'。

sentences.en: All I did was ask for a review.
sentences.cn: 我所做的只是要求复审。
sentences.structure: 主语（All I did）+ 系动词（was）+ 表语（ask for a review）
sentences.analysis: 主语从句 All I did 作主语，不定式短语作表语，省略了 to。

sentences.en: And you and I said to each other, oh, no, it looks like that is not happening.
sentences.cn: 你我都对彼此说，哦，不，看起来那不会发生。
sentences.structure: 主语（You and I）+ 谓语（said to each other）+ 直接引语（oh, no, it looks like that is not happening）
sentences.analysis: 直接引语中包含宾语从句 that is not happening 作 looks like 的宾语。

sentences.en: But what wasn't controversial was everybody understood that once the red card went down, he would be suspended to the next game.
sentences.cn: 但没有争议的是，每个人都明白一旦红牌出示，他将在下一场比赛中停赛。
sentences.structure: 主语从句（What wasn't controversial）+ 系动词（was）+ 表语从句（everybody understood that once the red card went down, he would be suspended to the next game）
sentences.analysis: 主语从句作主语；表语从句中 that 引导宾语从句，once 引导时间状语从句。

sentences.en: Certainly, a lot of people didn't like the call, but once the red card was given, there really wasn't a lot of debate whether or not he would be able to not play next game.
sentences.cn: 当然，很多人不喜欢这个判罚，但一旦红牌给出，对于他是否能在下一场比赛中上场，确实没有太多争论。
sentences.structure: 并列句：第一分句（a lot of people didn't like the call）+ 第二分句（but once the red card was given, there really wasn't a lot of debate whether or not he would be able to not play next game）
sentences.analysis: but 连接两个并列分句；第二分句中 once 引导时间状语从句，whether or not 引导同位语从句修饰 debate。

sentences.en: It was not up for appeal.
sentences.cn: 它不可上诉。
sentences.structure: 主语（It）+ 系动词（was）+ 表语（not up for appeal）
sentences.analysis: 固定短语 be up for 表示'可供……'。

sentences.en: He said, we are not just playing for the national team or the Federation.
sentences.cn: 他说，我们不仅仅是为国家队或足协而战。
sentences.structure: 主句（He said）+ 直接引语（we are not just playing for the national team or the Federation）
sentences.analysis: 直接引语作宾语。

sentences.en: We are playing for football itself.
sentences.cn: 我们是为足球本身而战。
sentences.structure: 主语（We）+ 谓语（are playing）+ 状语（for football itself）
sentences.analysis: 现在进行时表示正在进行的动作。

sentences.en: That's a lot for the U.S. team to come up against.
sentences.cn: 这对美国队来说是一个很大的挑战。
sentences.structure: 主语（That）+ 系动词（is）+ 表语（a lot）+ 定语（for the U.S. team to come up against）
sentences.analysis: 不定式复合结构作定语，修饰 a lot。

sentences.en: On the whole, the U.S. team looked like the moment was maybe a little bit too big for them, which you can kind of understand because it really was everyone against them for a team that is good, but not at the top tier of the sport.
sentences.cn: 总的来说，美国队看起来好像这个时刻对他们来说有点太大了，这你多少可以理解，因为对于一支优秀但并非顶级水平的球队来说，这真的是所有人都在与他们为敌。
sentences.structure: 主句（the U.S. team looked like the moment was maybe a little bit too big for them）+ 非限制性定语从句（which you can kind of understand）+ 原因状语从句（because it really was everyone against them for a team that is good, but not at the top tier of the sport）
sentences.analysis: looked like 后接宾语从句；which 指代整个主句；because 引导原因状语从句，其中 that 引导定语从句修饰 team。

sentences.en: They knew instantly they had gone from this fun underdog that everyone was cheering for and having these street parties cheering for to taking really tough questions and having the entire soccer world outside the United States, furious with them and rooting against them.
sentences.cn: 他们立刻意识到，他们从一个人人欢呼、街头派对的可爱弱者，变成了要面对非常棘手的问题，并且整个美国以外的足球世界都对他们愤怒、反对他们。
sentences.structure: 主句（They knew instantly）+ 宾语从句（they had gone from...to...）
sentences.analysis: 宾语从句中 from...to... 结构，from 后接名词短语，to 后接动名词短语；that 引导定语从句修饰 underdog。

sentences.en: They can feel that whether they will admit that or not, whether the state that was outside noise, they are part of the soccer world.
sentences.cn: 他们能感觉到，无论他们是否承认，无论外界噪音如何，他们都是足球世界的一部分。
sentences.structure: 主句（They can feel that）+ 宾语从句（whether they will admit that or not, whether the state that was outside noise, they are part of the soccer world）
sentences.analysis: 宾语从句中包含两个 whether 引导的让步状语从句，主句为 they are part of the soccer world。

sentences.en: So, certainly, there was a clear shift in the way that U.S. soccer was seen both before this and very much afterward, after kind of what Trump did.
sentences.cn: 所以，当然，在特朗普所做的一切之后，美国足球在人们眼中的看法发生了明显的转变，无论是在此之前还是之后。
sentences.structure: 主句（there was a clear shift in the way）+ 定语从句（that U.S. soccer was seen both before this and very much afterward）+ 时间状语（after kind of what Trump did）
sentences.analysis: that 引导定语从句修饰 way；what 引导宾语从句作 after 的宾语。

sentences.en: The one thing he's never crossed over to has been competition on the field.
sentences.cn: 他从未涉足的一件事就是场上的比赛。
sentences.structure: 主语（The one thing）+ 定语从句（he's never crossed over to）+ 系动词（has been）+ 表语（competition on the field）
sentences.analysis: 定语从句省略了关系代词 that/which，cross over to 表示'涉足'。

sentences.en: The integrity of the tournament itself has never been questioned until now.
sentences.cn: 比赛本身的完整性直到现在才受到质疑。
sentences.structure: 主语（The integrity of the tournament itself）+ 谓语（has never been questioned）+ 时间状语（until now）
sentences.analysis: 现在完成时的被动语态。

sentences.en: I think that comment is very telling about how Trump uses the sense that things were rigged to his political benefit.
sentences.cn: 我认为这句话很能说明特朗普如何利用事情被操纵的感觉来谋取他的政治利益。
sentences.structure: 主句（I think）+ 宾语从句（that comment is very telling about how Trump uses the sense that things were rigged to his political benefit）
sentences.analysis: 宾语从句中 how 引导宾语从句作 about 的宾语；that 引导同位语从句修饰 sense。

sentences.en: But I think what's really interesting here is, you know, we as Americans kind of look at this phone call to Gianni Infantino and we're kind of like, you know, that's how Trump operates.
sentences.cn: 但我认为真正有趣的是，你知道，我们美国人看待这通给詹尼·因凡蒂诺的电话，我们有点觉得，你知道，这就是特朗普的行事方式。
sentences.structure: 主句（I think）+ 宾语从句（what's really interesting here is...we as Americans kind of look at this phone call...and we're kind of like...that's how Trump operates）
sentences.analysis: 宾语从句中主语从句 what's really interesting here 作主语，系动词 is 后接表语从句，表语从句由并列句构成。

sentences.en: But I think when we look back at things like Trump talking about Greenland, talking about making Canada the 51st state, these were things that were offensive to the rest of the world.
sentences.cn: 但我认为，当我们回顾特朗普谈论格陵兰岛、谈论让加拿大成为第51个州之类的事情时，这些都是冒犯世界其他地方的事情。
sentences.structure: 主句（I think）+ 宾语从句（when we look back at things...these were things that were offensive to the rest of the world）
sentences.analysis: 宾语从句中 when 引导时间状语从句，主句为 these were things，that 引导定语从句修饰 things。

sentences.en: They created kind of international incidents that I don't think we in America really appreciated as much as other countries were experiencing.
sentences.cn: 它们制造了某种国际事件，我认为我们美国人并不像其他国家所经历的那样真正意识到这些事件。
sentences.structure: 主句（They created kind of international incidents）+ 定语从句（that I don't think we in America really appreciated as much as other countries were experiencing）
sentences.analysis: that 引导定语从句修饰 incidents；定语从句中 I don't think 是插入语，as much as 引导比较状语从句。

sentences.en: And I think this is kind of along those lines.
sentences.cn: 我认为这有点类似。
sentences.structure: 主句（I think）+ 宾语从句（this is kind of along those lines）
sentences.analysis: along those lines 是固定短语，表示'类似'。

sentences.en: These other countries are like, this guy is kind of, you know, throwing his weight around and creating unfair advantages.
sentences.cn: 这些其他国家觉得，这家伙有点，你知道，滥用职权，制造不公平的优势。
sentences.structure: 主句（These other countries are like）+ 直接引语（this guy is kind of...throwing his weight around and creating unfair advantages）
sentences.analysis: 直接引语作表语；throw one's weight around 是固定短语，表示'滥用职权'。

topics.title: 2026年世界杯美国队出局
topics.content: 美国队在2026年世界杯16强赛中以1-4输给比利时，被淘汰出局。赛前，美国球员弗拉林·巴洛贡因红牌面临停赛，但特朗普总统致电国际足联主席后，停赛被取消，引发国际争议。
topics.keywords: World Cup, U.S. soccer, Belgium, red card, Trump, FIFA

topics.title: 特朗普干预体育赛事争议
topics.content: 特朗普总统致电国际足联主席詹尼·因凡蒂诺，要求复审美国球员的红牌，导致停赛取消。此举被批评为利用政治权力干预体育公平，引发全球足球界愤怒，并被视为特朗普第二任期滥用职权的又一例证。
topics.keywords: Trump, FIFA, red card, political interference, sports integrity

topics.title: 国际足联腐败历史
topics.content: 国际足联（FIFA）曾多次卷入腐败丑闻，包括2022年贿赂丑闻。特朗普与因凡蒂诺的关系，以及因凡蒂诺讨好特朗普的行为，加剧了外界对FIFA独立性的质疑。
topics.keywords: FIFA, corruption, bribery, Gianni Infantino, Trump

topics.title: 北约峰会与乌克兰局势
topics.content: 特朗普抵达土耳其参加北约峰会，预计将与乌克兰总统泽连斯基会面。乌克兰面临俄罗斯导弹和无人机攻击，急需爱国者导弹防御系统。美国因同时支持以色列和乌克兰导致导弹拦截器短缺。
topics.keywords: NATO summit, Ukraine, Zelenskyy, Patriot missiles, Russia, Iran

topics.title: 土耳其与美国关系
topics.content: 特朗普与土耳其总统埃尔多安关系密切，土耳其可能重新获得F-35战斗机，引发以色列担忧。土耳其还曾阻止美国武装库尔德人，并在伊朗和叙利亚问题上扮演关键角色。
topics.keywords: Turkey, Erdogan, F-35, Israel, Kurds, Iran

topics.title: 美国政治与体育的交织
topics.content: 特朗普将体育事件政治化，如将世界杯失利与2020年选举舞弊指控类比。媒体对特朗普的过度关注被批评为“特朗普成瘾”，而体育界则呼吁保持体育的纯洁性，避免政治干预。
topics.keywords: Trump, media, politics, sports, World Cup, election rigging

raw_transcript:
Return to Transcripts main page

U.S. Out Of World Cup After Four-One Loss To Belgium; U.S. Out Of World Cup After Political Firestorm Over Suspension; President Trump Arrives In Turkey For NATO Summit; Aired 6:30-7a ET

Aired July 07, 2026 - 06:30   ET

THIS IS A RUSH TRANSCRIPT. THIS COPY MAY NOT BE IN ITS FINAL FORM AND MAY BE UPDATED.

AUDIE CORNISH, CNN HOST: Good morning, everybody. I'm Audie Cornish. Thank you for joining me on "CNN This Morning." It is half past the hour.

Here's what's happening right now. The man accused of placing pipe bombs in Washington, D.C. five years ago will not have his charges dropped. A judge on Monday rejected a motion to dismiss the case after the defendant tried to argue that Trump's sweeping pardon for January 6 rioters also applied to him. The judge says, the pardon does not apply because he had not yet been convicted of any crime when the pardon was issued.

A new video out of New Jersey showing the moment a roof of a supermarket collapsed two people were trapped under the debris. They were able to get out and there were no injuries. The roof collapsed after torrential rain in the area. Several roads were washed out by flood waters and the area hit with five inches of rain in just a few hours.

And Cuba working to restore power after suffering a nationwide blackout on Monday. The island has experienced several blackouts as the country's aging electric infrastructure struggles to meet demand. Cuba's energy crisis became worse after the U.S. issued a blockade on fuel shipments.

And the dream is over for the team USA in the World Cup Monday night. They lost to Belgium by a final score of four to one, eliminating them from the tournament in the Round of 16.

Now, the loss comes despite the fact that U.S. player, Folarin Balogun, was cleared to play, even though originally he was supposed to be suspended due to a red card.

And that suspension, of course, was lifted after a phone call from President Trump to the head of FIFA.

(BEGIN VIDEO CLIP) DONAD TRUMP, PRESIDENT OF THE UNITED STATES: He didn't do anything wrong, and he's our best player or one of our best players. A very vital player. And they gave him a red card. I didn't know what that meant. I didn't think it meant much. Then I started hearing that that means he can't play in the next game. At least in the next game.

All I did was ask for a review.

CORNISH: Joining me now, Will Leitch, contributing editor at "New York Magazine." Will, you and I have been talking for months. And it was like, is the U.S. going to become the super villain, the sports villain of the World Cup? Is Trump going to insert himself? And you and I said to each other, oh, no, it looks like that is not happening. People are eating barbecue and loving everything. And then, and then, and then.

So, was this a bad call?

WILL LEITCH, CONTRIBUTING EDITOR, NEW YORK MAGAZINE: We were so close.

CORNISH: We were so close. OK? But here I am.

So, when this was announced, the red card, was it considered a bad call? Was everyone in the World Cup like, oh, what a bummer the best player isn't going to make it? And we're OK with that call? Or was it controversial to start?

LEITCH: It was a controversial call to start. But what wasn't controversial was everybody understood that once the red card went down, he would be suspended to the next game. FIFA had no sense of appeal. That is the rule. That has been the rule throughout World Cups throughout history.

Certainly, a lot of people didn't like the call. I personally didn't like the call. But once the red card was given, there really wasn't a lot of debate whether or not he would be able to not play next game. It was not up for appeal. And yet, things didn't change in that regard.

CORNISH: There's a t-shirt around that jokingly says USA versus everybody. And then that t-shirt just became a reality because people started to freak out internationally about this.

Talk about the win from Belgium because they were clearly pleased with this victory.

LEITCH: Yes. It was quite interesting. Belgium's coach Rudi Garcia, before the game, they asked him how important this game was, which is a silly question. It's the World Cup qualifier. It's the World Cup game. Of course, it's a knockout phase. Of course, it's an important game.

He said, we are not just playing for the national team or the Federation. We are playing for football itself. That's a lot for the U.S. team to come up against. And you could see that really from the very beginning last night.

The U.S. really seemed -- the talent-wise, they were probably a little bit lower than Belgium. I think that that's a larger issue for U.S. soccer. But more to the point, they looked overwhelmed, kind of spooked from the beginning. They were very rattled from the start of this game. Belgium almost scored very early, didn't score very quickly.

On the whole, the U.S. team looked like the moment was maybe a little bit too big for them, which you can kind of understand because it really was everyone against them for a team that is good, but not at the top tier of the sport. It maybe was a little bit overwhelming point.

CORNISH: I want to play for you what the U.S. soccer players had to say after the match. We know what Belgium thinks because I think they tweeted overturn this, but here's what the players had to say.

TIM REAM, U.S. SOCCER DEFENDER: No. I had no idea. Outside noise, we've -- we've done a good job with this group of -- of allowing outside noise to be outside noise.

TYLER ADAMS, U.S. SOCCER MIDFIELDER: We were happy that we had an opportunity for him to play. I think he -- he tried today to -- to be a -- a presence and a nuisance, and at times, he was getting the ball in behind and -- and doing what he does. Just didn't have too many opportunities.

CORNISH: I can't help but wonder, like, was this a momentum killer? I mean, after the whole Trump-Knicks thing, I sort of, all the New Yorkers got it in my head that Trump's a cooler.

And did he basically hurt the vibes?

LEITCH: The vibes definitely shifted very quickly. This was an upstart team. You know, this has always -- U.S. soccer team is not generally like most U.S. national teams. They're not the overwhelming favorite. They're the plucky underdog. They're the way that Americans generally think we are, but aren't.

They -- they usually are that upstart team that you can get excited about. That switched so much on them. And certainly, you know, this was a team that everyone was behind, knew that everyone was behind, suddenly felt was getting tough questions from all across the board.

Certainly, these people -- the United States soccer players, they play internationally. They know what everyone is saying about this decision, everyone was saying about their team.

And they knew instantly they had gone from this fun underdog that everyone was cheering for and having these street parties cheering for to taking really tough questions and having the entire soccer world outside the United States, furious with them and rooting against them.

They can feel that whether -- whether they will admit that or not, whether the state that was outside noise, they are part of the soccer world. They know they play with the people. A lot of these people are teammates in their -- with their club teams with the people that were trying to beat them and they know how angry everybody is.

So, certainly, there was a clear shift in the way that U.S. soccer was seen both before this and very much afterward, after kind of what Trump did.

CORNISH: Will, I'm out of time, but I have to ask one last thing because FIFA's presidents looked this decision was made by an independent commission. The phone call from the man I gave a made-up peace prize to had nothing to do with it.

Can you talk to me about FIFA's Infantino? Is this a guy on the bubble? Or has he just done so much for so many countries who want a part of the FIFA revenue pie that it doesn't matter?

LEITCH: Certainly, this is the most perilous situation for him. He has made a lot of money for a lot of people. He's making a lot of money people -- for people from this World Cup.

But the one thing he's never crossed over to has been competition on the field. People have not loved everything he's done, but he's -- he's brought them a lot of money.

The tournament -- the integrity of the tournament itself has never been questioned until now. And I think that is -- I think it's provided an opening for people that were frustrated with him to potentially do something after this term. But you have to wonder if he will think it was really worth it when this tournament is over.

CORNISH: OK. Will, thanks so much. We'll have you back soon for the rest of the tournament. I got to bring in the group chat now to talk more about this because it's all of a sudden turned into a political story.

Aaron, your most recent CNN piece, you talked about how this kind of fits into a pattern for Trump in his second term. And your -- your story was in my mind because of this thing that Trump said. I want to play it for the audience.

TRUMP: And you know what? If they beat us, then they can be really proud. The other way, if they beat us, we'll say it was -- I say it was rigged. Just like the election was rigged in 2020.

CORNISH: This world sporting event is just like the election that I still insist despite no court agreeing with me was rigged. AARON BLAKE, CNN SENIOR POLITICAL REPORTER: I think that comment is very telling about how Trump uses the sense that things were rigged to his political benefit.

But I think what's really interesting here is --

CORNISH: But the pitch isn't politics. Like, somehow I feel like that falls flat in this completely different other context.

BLAKE: Yes, yes. No, I get that. And I think the interesting thing here is, you know, we as Americans kind of look at this -- this phone call to Gianni Infantino and we're kind of like, you know, that's how Trump operates. We're used to it by now.

But I think when we look back at things like Trump talking about Greenland, talking about making Canada the 51st state, these were things that were offensive to the rest of the world. They created kind of international incidents that I don't think we in America really appreciated as much as -- as other countries were experiencing.

And I think this is kind of along those lines. These other countries are like, this guy is kind of, you know, throwing his weight around and -- and creating unfair advantages. And -- and, you know, we're the victims of what this guy is doing right now.

CORNISH: Oh, can you stop at that phrase, unfair advantages? That's the thing I'm wondering about.

Marco Rubio, first of all, says everybody chill, from the secretary of state. This is not an international incident. Here he is.

MARCO RUBIO, U.S. SECRETARY OF STATE: Maybe turning into international incident. I don't know. Maybe we'll bring it up at NATO tomorrow when we're there with the Belgians and everybody else. But I just hope the match will go on. Everyone will be at full strength. And the winner will be the winner.

CORNISH: And I was thinking about this in the context of this polling from Pew Research Center. Has Trump improperly used his office to enrich himself or his family?

Sixty-one percent say, probably definitely. And that idea of unfair advantage. Is that like a ball rolling down the hill? Does it get bigger and bigger as more and more happens?

DOUG HEYE, REPUBLICAN STRATEGIST: Potentially so. And -- and, look, we see evidence of this all the time of what he's doing, what the family's doing.

But there's a separation between that, I think, in -- in the World Cup. And part of it is there's an addiction to all things Donald Trump. And I'm not a big defender of Donald Trump on the global stage. But Keir Starmer made a phone call to delay -- to try and delay or not delay a time for one of the matches. We've had other players.

HEYE: For -- for a -- a U.K., an England match.

HEYE: We've had other players.

CORNISH: Bringing suspended player back to the field?

HEYE: Well, but Cris -- but Cristiano Ronaldo had his -- had his suspension also --

HEYE: -- moved. So -- so this is precedented. The problem is the media has a pure dopamine Trump addiction. So, if we can talk about Donald Trump and make things about him, he loves it.

CORNISH: Let me let Karen jump in before we end.

HEYE: But let me say.

CORNISH: No, no, because I think one of the things about the media argument --

HEYE: The -- the Knicks game, it was more coverage about Donald Trump going to the Knicks game than the actual Knicks game. "The New York Times" is speaking.

KAREN FINNEY, CNN POLITICAL COMMENTATOR: Not -- that's not true.

HEYE: The lead piece on -- the lead opinion piece in "The New York Times" on July 4th was somebody said, I can't celebrate July 4th because of why? Donald Trump.

There was another story.

CORNISH: Doug, I'm going to put it out there. Under your face --

HEYE: What was the Taylor --

CORNISH: -- I'm going to put local man ignores sports fans --

CORNISH: -- and let your mentions would have fire. Because I would never --

HEYE: What -- what did "The New York Times" write about the Taylor Swift wedding? Well, gosh, it's got to be about Donald.

CORNISH: Knicks fans.

HEYE: Everything has to be about Donald.

CORNISH: Knicks fans were upset. That was a real thing.

HEYE: Are they still talking about it? No. The next day onto the next --

FINNEY: If you're a New Yorker, my brother, yes, you are. You are definitely (INAUDIBLE).

CORNISH: Which is fine. But what I'm saying is this is a collision of sports fandom and regular news. There's a reason why it's called the World Cup.

CORNISH: So, I don't really want to do one of those games of it's the media problem when this was international response by sports fans. And I think it's OK for them to respond. They don't have to be wrapped up in our bipartisan nonsense. And I do think he inserted himself.

HEYE: Of course, he did. And he knew that you would talk about him ad nauseam as you always do because you're addicted to him.

CORNISH: I'm not addicted.

HEYE: Break the addiction.

CORNISH: If you want to -- the World Cup is not a little league game, Doug. People were upset about this.

CORNISH: And I don't think --

HEYE: -- somehow "The New York Times" made Taylor's Swift's wedding about Donald Trump and Fourth of July about Donald Trump and the Knicks game about Donald Trump. The answer always goes back as he depends on you to do to Donald Trump.

FINNEY: I'm a little shocked that Doug, of all people, would spend how many minutes have we now been talking about Donald Trump?

Look, here's what I would say also as a former college athlete. It's -- there's something about the sanctity of sport, period, full stop. FIFA has had a lot of trouble in the last several decades with corruption. We know that.

And so -- and -- and Donald Trump has a problem with corruption. And so the call, it also played into a narrative, frankly, not just because of Trump, but because of FIFA. And because, as you pointed out, I made up this award to make Donald Trump happy.

But there is something about for athletes, about the sanctity of sports should be outside of politics with very rare exception, like, when Jimmy Carter had to make the call on the Olympics.

Generally, world leaders don't get involved. Politics does not get involved. And there's usually an attempt to keep it that way. And I think that's part of why people were so angry was, wait a second, why are you getting involved? And particularly when I'm going to say, I didn't even know what a red card was. I mean, come on.

CORNISH: Well, to be clear, FIFA 2022 bribery scandal, OK? So, just for you at home --

CORNISH: -- who were like FIFA scandal, if you're not following FIFA and it's OK if you aren't, they have been dealt many corruption blows in recent years. We were talking about Italy in the past.

So, this is something that is coming up and it becomes like everything with the World Cup symbolic.

BLAKE: Yes. And the pattern is super important here. If this was an isolated incident of President Trump calling a buddy and being like, hey, maybe you guys should review this, maybe that would be a little bit different.

But we are talking about FIFA which has this demonstrated the history of corruption issues. The United States government has gotten involved in prosecuting them for this.

We also have, as you mentioned the made-up Peace Prize. This is a FIFA president Gianni Infantino who has gone out of his way to curry favor with President Trump. There was a built-in power dynamic with those two that made that phone call not necessarily just a, can you please review this kind of phone call. It's understood kind of the -- the power dynamics that are involved in that. And so I think that's why this was a bigger deal than it might otherwise seem.

CORNISH: OK. We want to come back to domestic politics here. Turning to Maine, that Senate race, because Graham Platner very much in trouble and now reflecting on the best path forward, after new accusations, if he exits the Maine Senate race. Who should take his place? Will they have a better shot at defeating Susan Collins? We're going to talk about that in a minute.

And later on CNN, Trump wants to make your energy bill cheaper. Was defunding wind and solar the way to do it?

(COMMERCIAL BREAK) [06:50:53]

CORNISH: So, President Trump just landed in Turkey for the NATO summit, where tomorrow he is expected to meet with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy. In recent months, Trump has turned his focus away from Ukraine, instead focusing on Iran.

Now, attacks from Russia this week, ramping up. Yesterday, 19 people were killed in Kyiv. Zelenskyy plans to ask the U.S. and European allies to deliver air defense systems that urgently needs to protect against Russia's escalating attacks.

VOLODYMYR ZELENSKYY, UKRAINIAN PRESIDENT (through translator): Unfortunately, there is a shortage of anti-ballistic capabilities, a shortage of patriots, and our partners, primarily the United States and our European partners, need to be more proactive in providing assistance with us.

CORNISH: OK. Bringing in CNN national security analyst Alex Plitsas. And, Alex, right now, we are looking at the president's plane landing in Ankara, Turkey. We know that Ukraine has a lot of questions going into this summit.

And months ago, president said to Zelenskyy, you don't hold the cards. Have things changed for Ukraine, especially in the light of the -- of the war with Iran, where all of a sudden knowledge about how to conduct drone warfare was very valuable?

ALEX PLITSAS, CNN NATIONAL SECURITY ANALYST: So, things have fundamentally changed on the battlefield. And quite frankly, I'm told, so has the president's perception of the war, and that Ukraine has been successful now in long-range strike capabilities in hitting Russian oil and gas to make sure that Putin couldn't take advantage of the war in Iran with rising fuel prices to make money, but also causing domestic fuel shortages at home.

The second is the staggering number of casualties that Ukraine has been able to inflict. They were trying to get about 50,000 a month. We're now told by British intelligence that number sits at around 500,000. That's out of a population of 143 million.

So you're talking one in every 287 Russian citizens. And because the population of military-age males is only about 21 million, I mean, you're talking with one in almost 42 males of that age group has died in Ukraine. That's simply unsustainable. And Putin's going to have to launch a mobilization, given the losses if he wants to spring offensive.

CORNISH: How would Patriot missiles make the difference? PLITSAS: So, his response has been to continue to launch ballistic missiles at -- at Kyiv, as well as these long-range drones. And quite frankly, they're running short on interceptors. So, President Zelenskyy is asking NATO allies, particularly the United States, to provide those systems.

But as you correctly noted, we've had to provide defensive capabilities because of the war in Iran to both -- to both Israel and then also to Ukraine and then lead up to both of those wars.

Some of those missile defense systems were going end-of-life in the United States and projections about how many we would need and for how long and when the new systems would come, et cetera, was all predicated upon usage and nobody foresaw this war with Ukraine or the wars that Israel launched in Gaza or having to defend against, you know, Iran on the -- on the horizon. Unfortunately, that meant a shortage of interceptor systems.

CORNISH: So, this plane is landing in Ankara. Can you talk to me about what it is European allies want out of Trump? Is this just their usual kind of management trying to prove that they're doing more spending when it comes to NATO? Or do they have real asks around either the Russian war or what's going on with Iran?

PLITSAS: So, it's a mix of everything you just described. So, yes, they would like to see more U.S. involvement and support for Ukraine. They do want to tout increased spending. The president made it part of his agenda that he wanted an increase in spending from NATO states from three percent of GDP to five percent, which is actually now why we're seeing the U.S. try to propose a budget of 1.5 trillion.

The messaging hasn't been great around that, but that's about five percent of U.S. GDP, which is what we're asking the Europeans to spend on that.

And then lastly, I was told that he is in Turkey personally because that President Erdogan asked him to come and this surrounds, you know, Turkey's involvement in a number of issues. They helped broker some of the backdoor agreements with Iran. They also played a key role in Syria and helping to stabilize there as far as the president's concern.

But the Israelis are apoplectic. They see Erdogan's language in Turkey as a long-term strategic threat. And so what looms large over this inside the discussions is a U.S. decision to give or to allow Turkey to participate in the F-35 program again to give it that advanced fighter jet, which the Israelis are worried will tip the balance of power in the region.

CORNISH: You know, it's interesting right now, we're watching a carpet being rolled out into Air Force One.

And as much as this is a summit, when it comes to Trump, we're talking about bilateral relationships, right? He's got a relationship with Netanyahu. He's got a relationship with Erdogan.

What do you know about the balance of these two kind of who's up and who's down and in terms of his favor?

PLITSAS: So, Turkey is definitely on the rise in terms of favoritism. I mean, he -- President Trump and President Erdogan's relationship has continued to grow over time. And Erdogan has used that to his advantage.

You know, there was, I was told, of actual plan to arm the Kurds at one point. It was part of a long-term Israeli plan to eventually, you know, see the overthrow of the Iranian government.

But Erdogan stepped in because he didn't want to see another independent or semi-autonomous Kurdish state like we have in -- in Northern Iraq.

Because about a third of Eastern Turkey is Kurdish and they fought a long-term war against the PKK, which is a Turkish separatist group that's a designated terrorist organization.

So, he was worried about that. He stepped in, used it for that. He's able to -- to maneuver to get the F-35 that he wanted. And he and President Trump just have this relationship just given their styles. And it has really unnerved the Israelis.

CORNISH: OK. Alex, thanks so much. Always love your insight. Appreciate that.

Right now, you are looking at the President's plane, which has touched down in Turkey for a meeting with NATO allies.

Turkey really wanted the president there. We've been talking about that relationship.

I want to turn to the group chat because this is a kind of day where we just want to know, what do you think is going to come out of this meeting today? Doug, let me start with you. Is there a particular leader you're watching coming out of this?

HEYE: I'm looking to see what the side conversations are. There's been a real effort over the past year and a half. I was at some of the meetings for this last year for what's called the Defense Security and Resilience Bank that would basically help fund those -- those countries who haven't gotten to the five percent threshold yet. Will that be successful? Will that rise in -- in the attention of the leaders?

CORNISH: Go ahead, Karen.

FINNEY: Yes. I'm looking for just the tone and tenor because one of the things we're seeing are -- is the European leaders, like, other parts of the world, kind of figuring out how to get along without us because the United States has become less reliable.

And so some of those dynamics I think will be in play in terms of the posture of some of these leaders.

CORNISH: Now, Aaron, you and I were nerding out about the sort of weapons systems and questions around Air Force support that people will be watching for here in military land, but can you talk to me about what else you're watching?

BLAKE: Yes. The -- the decoupling is a big thing. You know, we have that big speech from --

BLAKE: Decoupling. The Mark Carney speech in Brussels earlier this year where he kind of laid out a path forward for these Western countries to maybe not be so closely aligned with the United States.

Then also, I think this F-35 issue is really interesting, not just because of the relationship with Israel, like we talked about, on Netanyahu does not want these -- these -- the stealth technology going to Turkey, but also Congress might not like this.

Congress could stand in the way if Trump wants to press forward with this. That's a very interesting dynamic in Washington.

CORNISH: OK. A lot of questions going into the day. Thank you to your -- to our group chat. Really appreciate you guys being here. Thank you to you for waking up with us.

Right now, we are watching the president touchdown interview where he is set to meet with NATO allies to talk about Ukraine, among other things.

I'm Audie Cornish. And I want you to stay with us because we've got these headlines next.

segments.title: CNN.com - Transcripts
segments.text:
Return to Transcripts main page

U.S. Out Of World Cup After Four-One Loss To Belgium; U.S. Out Of World Cup After Political Firestorm Over Suspension; President Trump Arrives In Turkey For NATO Summit; Aired 6:30-7a ET

Aired July 07, 2026 - 06:30   ET

THIS IS A RUSH TRANSCRIPT. THIS COPY MAY NOT BE IN ITS FINAL FORM AND MAY BE UPDATED.

AUDIE CORNISH, CNN HOST: Good morning, everybody. I'm Audie Cornish. Thank you for joining me on "CNN This Morning." It is half past the hour.

Here's what's happening right now. The man accused of placing pipe bombs in Washington, D.C. five years ago will not have his charges dropped. A judge on Monday rejected a motion to dismiss the case after the defendant tried to argue that Trump's sweeping pardon for January 6 rioters also applied to him. The judge says, the pardon does not apply because he had not yet been convicted of any crime when the pardon was issued.

A new video out of New Jersey showing the moment a roof of a supermarket collapsed two people were trapped under the debris. They were able to get out and there were no injuries. The roof collapsed after torrential rain in the area. Several roads were washed out by flood waters and the area hit with five inches of rain in just a few hours.

And Cuba working to restore power after suffering a nationwide blackout on Monday. The island has experienced several blackouts as the country's aging electric infrastructure struggles to meet demand. Cuba's energy crisis became worse after the U.S. issued a blockade on fuel shipments.

And the dream is over for the team USA in the World Cup Monday night. They lost to Belgium by a final score of four to one, eliminating them from the tournament in the Round of 16.

Now, the loss comes despite the fact that U.S. player, Folarin Balogun, was cleared to play, even though originally he was supposed to be suspended due to a red card.

And that suspension, of course, was lifted after a phone call from President Trump to the head of FIFA.

(BEGIN VIDEO CLIP) DONAD TRUMP, PRESIDENT OF THE UNITED STATES: He didn't do anything wrong, and he's our best player or one of our best players. A very vital player. And they gave him a red card. I didn't know what that meant. I didn't think it meant much. Then I started hearing that that means he can't play in the next game. At least in the next game.

All I did was ask for a review.

CORNISH: Joining me now, Will Leitch, contributing editor at "New York Magazine." Will, you and I have been talking for months. And it was like, is the U.S. going to become the super villain, the sports villain of the World Cup? Is Trump going to insert himself? And you and I said to each other, oh, no, it looks like that is not happening. People are eating barbecue and loving everything. And then, and then, and then.

So, was this a bad call?

WILL LEITCH, CONTRIBUTING EDITOR, NEW YORK MAGAZINE: We were so close.

CORNISH: We were so close. OK? But here I am.

So, when this was announced, the red card, was it considered a bad call? Was everyone in the World Cup like, oh, what a bummer the best player isn't going to make it? And we're OK with that call? Or was it controversial to start?

LEITCH: It was a controversial call to start. But what wasn't controversial was everybody understood that once the red card went down, he would be suspended to the next game. FIFA had no sense of appeal. That is the rule. That has been the rule throughout World Cups throughout history.

Certainly, a lot of people didn't like the call. I personally didn't like the call. But once the red card was given, there really wasn't a lot of debate whether or not he would be able to not play next game. It was not up for appeal. And yet, things didn't change in that regard.

CORNISH: There's a t-shirt around that jokingly says USA versus everybody. And then that t-shirt just became a reality because people started to freak out internationally about this.

Talk about the win from Belgium because they were clearly pleased with this victory.

LEITCH: Yes. It was quite interesting. Belgium's coach Rudi Garcia, before the game, they asked him how important this game was, which is a silly question. It's the World Cup qualifier. It's the World Cup game. Of course, it's a knockout phase. Of course, it's an important game.

He said, we are not just playing for the national team or the Federation. We are playing for football itself. That's a lot for the U.S. team to come up against. And you could see that really from the very beginning last night.

The U.S. really seemed -- the talent-wise, they were probably a little bit lower than Belgium. I think that that's a larger issue for U.S. soccer. But more to the point, they looked overwhelmed, kind of spooked from the beginning. They were very rattled from the start of this game. Belgium almost scored very early, didn't score very quickly.

On the whole, the U.S. team looked like the moment was maybe a little bit too big for them, which you can kind of understand because it really was everyone against them for a team that is good, but not at the top tier of the sport. It maybe was a little bit overwhelming point.

CORNISH: I want to play for you what the U.S. soccer players had to say after the match. We know what Belgium thinks because I think they tweeted overturn this, but here's what the players had to say.

TIM REAM, U.S. SOCCER DEFENDER: No. I had no idea. Outside noise, we've -- we've done a good job with this group of -- of allowing outside noise to be outside noise.

TYLER ADAMS, U.S. SOCCER MIDFIELDER: We were happy that we had an opportunity for him to play. I think he -- he tried today to -- to be a -- a presence and a nuisance, and at times, he was getting the ball in behind and -- and doing what he does. Just didn't have too many opportunities.

CORNISH: I can't help but wonder, like, was this a momentum killer? I mean, after the whole Trump-Knicks thing, I sort of, all the New Yorkers got it in my head that Trump's a cooler.

And did he basically hurt the vibes?

LEITCH: The vibes definitely shifted very quickly. This was an upstart team. You know, this has always -- U.S. soccer team is not generally like most U.S. national teams. They're not the overwhelming favorite. They're the plucky underdog. They're the way that Americans generally think we are, but aren't.

They -- they usually are that upstart team that you can get excited about. That switched so much on them. And certainly, you know, this was a team that everyone was behind, knew that everyone was behind, suddenly felt was getting tough questions from all across the board.

Certainly, these people -- the United States soccer players, they play internationally. They know what everyone is saying about this decision, everyone was saying about their team.

And they knew instantly they had gone from this fun underdog that everyone was cheering for and having these street parties cheering for to taking really tough questions and having the entire soccer world outside the United States, furious with them and rooting against them.

They can feel that whether -- whether they will admit that or not, whether the state that was outside noise, they are part of the soccer world. They know they play with the people. A lot of these people are teammates in their -- with their club teams with the people that were trying to beat them and they know how angry everybody is.

So, certainly, there was a clear shift in the way that U.S. soccer was seen both before this and very much afterward, after kind of what Trump did.

CORNISH: Will, I'm out of time, but I have to ask one last thing because FIFA's presidents looked this decision was made by an independent commission. The phone call from the man I gave a made-up peace prize to had nothing to do with it.

Can you talk to me about FIFA's Infantino? Is this a guy on the bubble? Or has he just done so much for so many countries who want a part of the FIFA revenue pie that it doesn't matter?

LEITCH: Certainly, this is the most perilous situation for him. He has made a lot of money for a lot of people. He's making a lot of money people -- for people from this World Cup.

But the one thing he's never crossed over to has been competition on the field. People have not loved everything he's done, but he's -- he's brought them a lot of money.

The tournament -- the integrity of the tournament itself has never been questioned until now. And I think that is -- I think it's provided an opening for people that were frustrated with him to potentially do something after this term. But you have to wonder if he will think it was really worth it when this tournament is over.

CORNISH: OK. Will, thanks so much. We'll have you back soon for the rest of the tournament. I got to bring in the group chat now to talk more about this because it's all of a sudden turned into a political story.

Aaron, your most recent CNN piece, you talked about how this kind of fits into a pattern for Trump in his second term. And your -- your story was in my mind because of this thing that Trump said. I want to play it for the audience.

TRUMP: And you know what? If they beat us, then they can be really proud. The other way, if they beat us, we'll say it was -- I say it was rigged. Just like the election was rigged in 2020.

CORNISH: This world sporting event is just like the election that I still insist despite no court agreeing with me was rigged. AARON BLAKE, CNN SENIOR POLITICAL REPORTER: I think that comment is very telling about how Trump uses the sense that things were rigged to his political benefit.

But I think what's really interesting here is --

CORNISH: But the pitch isn't politics. Like, somehow I feel like that falls flat in this completely different other context.

BLAKE: Yes, yes. No, I get that. And I think the interesting thing here is, you know, we as Americans kind of look at this -- this phone call to Gianni Infantino and we're kind of like, you know, that's how Trump operates. We're used to it by now.

But I think when we look back at things like Trump talking about Greenland, talking about making Canada the 51st state, these were things that were offensive to the rest of the world. They created kind of international incidents that I don't think we in America really appreciated as much as -- as other countries were experiencing.

And I think this is kind of along those lines. These other countries are like, this guy is kind of, you know, throwing his weight around and -- and creating unfair advantages. And -- and, you know, we're the victims of what this guy is doing right now.

CORNISH: Oh, can you stop at that phrase, unfair advantages? That's the thing I'm wondering about.

Marco Rubio, first of all, says everybody chill, from the secretary of state. This is not an international incident. Here he is.

MARCO RUBIO, U.S. SECRETARY OF STATE: Maybe turning into international incident. I don't know. Maybe we'll bring it up at NATO tomorrow when we're there with the Belgians and everybody else. But I just hope the match will go on. Everyone will be at full strength. And the winner will be the winner.

CORNISH: And I was thinking about this in the context of this polling from Pew Research Center. Has Trump improperly used his office to enrich himself or his family?

Sixty-one percent say, probably definitely. And that idea of unfair advantage. Is that like a ball rolling down the hill? Does it get bigger and bigger as more and more happens?

DOUG HEYE, REPUBLICAN STRATEGIST: Potentially so. And -- and, look, we see evidence of this all the time of what he's doing, what the family's doing.

But there's a separation between that, I think, in -- in the World Cup. And part of it is there's an addiction to all things Donald Trump. And I'm not a big defender of Donald Trump on the global stage. But Keir Starmer made a phone call to delay -- to try and delay or not delay a time for one of the matches. We've had other players.

HEYE: For -- for a -- a U.K., an England match.

HEYE: We've had other players.

CORNISH: Bringing suspended player back to the field?

HEYE: Well, but Cris -- but Cristiano Ronaldo had his -- had his suspension also --

HEYE: -- moved. So -- so this is precedented. The problem is the media has a pure dopamine Trump addiction. So, if we can talk about Donald Trump and make things about him, he loves it.

CORNISH: Let me let Karen jump in before we end.

HEYE: But let me say.

CORNISH: No, no, because I think one of the things about the media argument --

HEYE: The -- the Knicks game, it was more coverage about Donald Trump going to the Knicks game than the actual Knicks game. "The New York Times" is speaking.

KAREN FINNEY, CNN POLITICAL COMMENTATOR: Not -- that's not true.

HEYE: The lead piece on -- the lead opinion piece in "The New York Times" on July 4th was somebody said, I can't celebrate July 4th because of why? Donald Trump.

There was another story.

CORNISH: Doug, I'm going to put it out there. Under your face --

HEYE: What was the Taylor --

CORNISH: -- I'm going to put local man ignores sports fans --

CORNISH: -- and let your mentions would have fire. Because I would never --

HEYE: What -- what did "The New York Times" write about the Taylor Swift wedding? Well, gosh, it's got to be about Donald.

CORNISH: Knicks fans.

HEYE: Everything has to be about Donald.

CORNISH: Knicks fans were upset. That was a real thing.

HEYE: Are they still talking about it? No. The next day onto the next --

FINNEY: If you're a New Yorker, my brother, yes, you are. You are definitely (INAUDIBLE).

CORNISH: Which is fine. But what I'm saying is this is a collision of sports fandom and regular news. There's a reason why it's called the World Cup.

CORNISH: So, I don't really want to do one of those games of it's the media problem when this was international response by sports fans. And I think it's OK for them to respond. They don't have to be wrapped up in our bipartisan nonsense. And I do think he inserted himself.

HEYE: Of course, he did. And he knew that you would talk about him ad nauseam as you always do because you're addicted to him.

CORNISH: I'm not addicted.

HEYE: Break the addiction.

CORNISH: If you want to -- the World Cup is not a little league game, Doug. People were upset about this.

CORNISH: And I don't think --

HEYE: -- somehow "The New York Times" made Taylor's Swift's wedding about Donald Trump and Fourth of July about Donald Trump and the Knicks game about Donald Trump. The answer always goes back as he depends on you to do to Donald Trump.

FINNEY: I'm a little shocked that Doug, of all people, would spend how many minutes have we now been talking about Donald Trump?

Look, here's what I would say also as a former college athlete. It's -- there's something about the sanctity of sport, period, full stop. FIFA has had a lot of trouble in the last several decades with corruption. We know that.

And so -- and -- and Donald Trump has a problem with corruption. And so the call, it also played into a narrative, frankly, not just because of Trump, but because of FIFA. And because, as you pointed out, I made up this award to make Donald Trump happy.

But there is something about for athletes, about the sanctity of sports should be outside of politics with very rare exception, like, when Jimmy Carter had to make the call on the Olympics.

Generally, world leaders don't get involved. Politics does not get involved. And there's usually an attempt to keep it that way. And I think that's part of why people were so angry was, wait a second, why are you getting involved? And particularly when I'm going to say, I didn't even know what a red card was. I mean, come on.

CORNISH: Well, to be clear, FIFA 2022 bribery scandal, OK? So, just for you at home --

CORNISH: -- who were like FIFA scandal, if you're not following FIFA and it's OK if you aren't, they have been dealt many corruption blows in recent years. We were talking about Italy in the past.

So, this is something that is coming up and it becomes like everything with the World Cup symbolic.

BLAKE: Yes. And the pattern is super important here. If this was an isolated incident of President Trump calling a buddy and being like, hey, maybe you guys should review this, maybe that would be a little bit different.

But we are talking about FIFA which has this demonstrated the history of corruption issues. The United States government has gotten involved in prosecuting them for this.

We also have, as you mentioned the made-up Peace Prize. This is a FIFA president Gianni Infantino who has gone out of his way to curry favor with President Trump. There was a built-in power dynamic with those two that made that phone call not necessarily just a, can you please review this kind of phone call. It's understood kind of the -- the power dynamics that are involved in that. And so I think that's why this was a bigger deal than it might otherwise seem.

CORNISH: OK. We want to come back to domestic politics here. Turning to Maine, that Senate race, because Graham Platner very much in trouble and now reflecting on the best path forward, after new accusations, if he exits the Maine Senate race. Who should take his place? Will they have a better shot at defeating Susan Collins? We're going to talk about that in a minute.

And later on CNN, Trump wants to make your energy bill cheaper. Was defunding wind and solar the way to do it?

(COMMERCIAL BREAK) [06:50:53]

CORNISH: So, President Trump just landed in Turkey for the NATO summit, where tomorrow he is expected to meet with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy. In recent months, Trump has turned his focus away from Ukraine, instead focusing on Iran.

Now, attacks from Russia this week, ramping up. Yesterday, 19 people were killed in Kyiv. Zelenskyy plans to ask the U.S. and European allies to deliver air defense systems that urgently needs to protect against Russia's escalating attacks.

VOLODYMYR ZELENSKYY, UKRAINIAN PRESIDENT (through translator): Unfortunately, there is a shortage of anti-ballistic capabilities, a shortage of patriots, and our partners, primarily the United States and our European partners, need to be more proactive in providing assistance with us.

CORNISH: OK. Bringing in CNN national security analyst Alex Plitsas. And, Alex, right now, we are looking at the president's plane landing in Ankara, Turkey. We know that Ukraine has a lot of questions going into this summit.

And months ago, president said to Zelenskyy, you don't hold the cards. Have things changed for Ukraine, especially in the light of the -- of the war with Iran, where all of a sudden knowledge about how to conduct drone warfare was very valuable?

ALEX PLITSAS, CNN NATIONAL SECURITY ANALYST: So, things have fundamentally changed on the battlefield. And quite frankly, I'm told, so has the president's perception of the war, and that Ukraine has been successful now in long-range strike capabilities in hitting Russian oil and gas to make sure that Putin couldn't take advantage of the war in Iran with rising fuel prices to make money, but also causing domestic fuel shortages at home.

The second is the staggering number of casualties that Ukraine has been able to inflict. They were trying to get about 50,000 a month. We're now told by British intelligence that number sits at around 500,000. That's out of a population of 143 million.

So you're talking one in every 287 Russian citizens. And because the population of military-age males is only about 21 million, I mean, you're talking with one in almost 42 males of that age group has died in Ukraine. That's simply unsustainable. And Putin's going to have to launch a mobilization, given the losses if he wants to spring offensive.

CORNISH: How would Patriot missiles make the difference? PLITSAS: So, his response has been to continue to launch ballistic missiles at -- at Kyiv, as well as these long-range drones. And quite frankly, they're running short on interceptors. So, President Zelenskyy is asking NATO allies, particularly the United States, to provide those systems.

But as you correctly noted, we've had to provide defensive capabilities because of the war in Iran to both -- to both Israel and then also to Ukraine and then lead up to both of those wars.

Some of those missile defense systems were going end-of-life in the United States and projections about how many we would need and for how long and when the new systems would come, et cetera, was all predicated upon usage and nobody foresaw this war with Ukraine or the wars that Israel launched in Gaza or having to defend against, you know, Iran on the -- on the horizon. Unfortunately, that meant a shortage of interceptor systems.

CORNISH: So, this plane is landing in Ankara. Can you talk to me about what it is European allies want out of Trump? Is this just their usual kind of management trying to prove that they're doing more spending when it comes to NATO? Or do they have real asks around either the Russian war or what's going on with Iran?

PLITSAS: So, it's a mix of everything you just described. So, yes, they would like to see more U.S. involvement and support for Ukraine. They do want to tout increased spending. The president made it part of his agenda that he wanted an increase in spending from NATO states from three percent of GDP to five percent, which is actually now why we're seeing the U.S. try to propose a budget of 1.5 trillion.

The messaging hasn't been great around that, but that's about five percent of U.S. GDP, which is what we're asking the Europeans to spend on that.

And then lastly, I was told that he is in Turkey personally because that President Erdogan asked him to come and this surrounds, you know, Turkey's involvement in a number of issues. They helped broker some of the backdoor agreements with Iran. They also played a key role in Syria and helping to stabilize there as far as the president's concern.

But the Israelis are apoplectic. They see Erdogan's language in Turkey as a long-term strategic threat. And so what looms large over this inside the discussions is a U.S. decision to give or to allow Turkey to participate in the F-35 program again to give it that advanced fighter jet, which the Israelis are worried will tip the balance of power in the region.

CORNISH: You know, it's interesting right now, we're watching a carpet being rolled out into Air Force One.

And as much as this is a summit, when it comes to Trump, we're talking about bilateral relationships, right? He's got a relationship with Netanyahu. He's got a relationship with Erdogan.

What do you know about the balance of these two kind of who's up and who's down and in terms of his favor?

PLITSAS: So, Turkey is definitely on the rise in terms of favoritism. I mean, he -- President Trump and President Erdogan's relationship has continued to grow over time. And Erdogan has used that to his advantage.

You know, there was, I was told, of actual plan to arm the Kurds at one point. It was part of a long-term Israeli plan to eventually, you know, see the overthrow of the Iranian government.

But Erdogan stepped in because he didn't want to see another independent or semi-autonomous Kurdish state like we have in -- in Northern Iraq.

Because about a third of Eastern Turkey is Kurdish and they fought a long-term war against the PKK, which is a Turkish separatist group that's a designated terrorist organization.

So, he was worried about that. He stepped in, used it for that. He's able to -- to maneuver to get the F-35 that he wanted. And he and President Trump just have this relationship just given their styles. And it has really unnerved the Israelis.

CORNISH: OK. Alex, thanks so much. Always love your insight. Appreciate that.

Right now, you are looking at the President's plane, which has touched down in Turkey for a meeting with NATO allies.

Turkey really wanted the president there. We've been talking about that relationship.

I want to turn to the group chat because this is a kind of day where we just want to know, what do you think is going to come out of this meeting today? Doug, let me start with you. Is there a particular leader you're watching coming out of this?

HEYE: I'm looking to see what the side conversations are. There's been a real effort over the past year and a half. I was at some of the meetings for this last year for what's called the Defense Security and Resilience Bank that would basically help fund those -- those countries who haven't gotten to the five percent threshold yet. Will that be successful? Will that rise in -- in the attention of the leaders?

CORNISH: Go ahead, Karen.

FINNEY: Yes. I'm looking for just the tone and tenor because one of the things we're seeing are -- is the European leaders, like, other parts of the world, kind of figuring out how to get along without us because the United States has become less reliable.

And so some of those dynamics I think will be in play in terms of the posture of some of these leaders.

CORNISH: Now, Aaron, you and I were nerding out about the sort of weapons systems and questions around Air Force support that people will be watching for here in military land, but can you talk to me about what else you're watching?

BLAKE: Yes. The -- the decoupling is a big thing. You know, we have that big speech from --

BLAKE: Decoupling. The Mark Carney speech in Brussels earlier this year where he kind of laid out a path forward for these Western countries to maybe not be so closely aligned with the United States.

Then also, I think this F-35 issue is really interesting, not just because of the relationship with Israel, like we talked about, on Netanyahu does not want these -- these -- the stealth technology going to Turkey, but also Congress might not like this.

Congress could stand in the way if Trump wants to press forward with this. That's a very interesting dynamic in Washington.

CORNISH: OK. A lot of questions going into the day. Thank you to your -- to our group chat. Really appreciate you guys being here. Thank you to you for waking up with us.

Right now, we are watching the president touchdown interview where he is set to meet with NATO allies to talk about Ukraine, among other things.

I'm Audie Cornish. And I want you to stay with us because we've got these headlines next.
